params.text:
Ways people plan ahead

The MCA gives people legal tools to shape decisions before they might lose capacity, and provides advocacy for those with no-one to speak for them.

Lasting Power of Attorney (LPA)

An LPA lets a person (the ‘donor’) appoint one or more trusted people (‘attorneys’) to make decisions if they later lose capacity. There are two separate types:

  • Health and welfare LPA — covers decisions about care, medical treatment and where the person lives. It can only be used once the donor has lost capacity, and only covers life-sustaining treatment if the donor expressly said so.
  • Property and financial affairs LPA — covers money, bills, benefits and property. It can be used while the donor still has capacity, if they agree.

An LPA is only valid once registered with the Office of the Public Guardian. If someone tells you they are an attorney, it is reasonable to ask to see the registered document.

params.alt: Illustration for Planning ahead: LPA, ADRT & IMCA

params.text:
Advance decisions and independent advocacy

Advance decision to refuse treatment (ADRT): a person aged 18 or over with capacity can set out, in advance, specific medical treatments they wish to refuse in the future should they lose capacity. A valid and applicable ADRT is legally binding on health professionals. To refuse life-sustaining treatment, the ADRT must be in writing, signed and witnessed, and must clearly state it applies even if life is at risk. Note that an advance decision can only refuse treatment — it cannot demand a particular treatment.

Independent Mental Capacity Advocate (IMCA): an IMCA must be instructed when a person who lacks capacity has no-one appropriate (other than paid staff) to consult, and a decision is being made about serious medical treatment or a change of accommodation arranged by the NHS or local authority. The IMCA represents and supports the person, gathers information and makes sure their rights and wishes are properly considered.

params.question: Frank has capacity now and wants to make sure that, if he later develops advanced dementia, he can refuse a specific life-sustaining treatment. Which tool should he use, and what must it meet to be valid?

answers.text: An advance decision to refuse treatment (ADRT), in writing, signed and witnessed, clearly stating it applies even if his life is at risk.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Refusing life-sustaining treatment in advance requires a valid ADRT that is written, signed, witnessed and explicitly states it applies even where life is at risk.

answers.text: A property and financial affairs LPA, because it lets his attorney handle everything.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. A property and financial affairs LPA covers money and property, not medical treatment decisions.

answers.text: A verbal statement to a nurse is enough, as long as two people hear it.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. To refuse life-sustaining treatment, the advance decision must be in writing, signed and witnessed, and must state it applies even if life is at risk.

answers.text: An IMCA referral, because IMCAs make treatment decisions for people.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. An IMCA supports and represents a person who has no-one else to consult — they do not make the decision, and this is not a planning-ahead tool.
